title: Copy, Replace, Rename, Delete, Batch, and Concurrency · Runtime Contract: Conditional Write Conflict
summary: A rename is presented as atomic, replacement loses metadata, delete hides a live version, and concurrent writers silently overwrite each other. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the conditional write conflict increment in conditional object-mutation protocol. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Use validators, version IDs, idempotency records, or application locks to detect stale concurrent mutation and expose 409 or 412 meaning.
Implement rename as version-aware copy, validation, metadata update, reference switch, and safe source deletion with recoverable intermediate states.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Conditional Write Conflict · Theory Conditional Write Conflict
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 9754 during a high-traffic evidence intake window. Build deterministic Go evidence to model a non-atomic version-aware copy, validate, and delete rename protocol; record cleanup, rollback, restoration, and residual-risk ownership; then double concurrent transfers and keep process resource limits intact. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Conditional Write Conflict contract for the supplied data.

Evidence target: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Conditional Write Conflict · Theory Conditional Write Conflict.
